cnidarians text index | photo index Phylum Cnidaria

Class Anthozoa > Order Actiniaria

Photo index of cnidarians on Singapore shores sea anemones and anemone-like animals
polyp diameter < 3cm

Size given is the diameter with tentacles expanded


Banded bead
anemone
awaiting identification


Striped
bead anemone
awaiting identification


Hermit crab
anemone
awaiting identification


Posy
anemone
awaiting identification


Mini carpet
anemone
Stichodactyal tapetum

1-2cm. One row of tapered tentacles with a banded pattern. Large oral relative to tentacles. Usually brownish. On rocky shores, in sand or in cracks in large boulders and sea walls. Commonly seen on many of our shores. 2-3cm. Abut 40 tentacles with dark stripes and white bars. On hard surfaces and rocky shores. Common on our Northern shores. 1-2cm. Short tapering tentacles. Sometimes more than one anemone on a hermit crab shell. Also seen on living whelks. Sometimes seen on many of our shores. 2-4cm. Short tentacles (about 2cm) in 2-3 rings around a large oral disk. Coral rubble. Seen in large groups on Pulau Sekudu, singly or smaller groups on other shore. 2-6cm. Very short tentacles like bumps, arranged like a wheel near the centre. On seagrasses on our Northern shore.


Sea mat zoanthids Platyhoa tuberculosa


Broad disk zoanthids Platyhoa mutuki


Button zoanthids Zoanthus sp.

Colony 20-40cm, encrusts rocks and rubble like a rubbery mat. Polyps 1-2cm in diameter embedded in this common tissue, short or no body column. Coral rubble, boulders. Commonly seen on many of our Southern shores.

Colony 5-10cm, a cluster of a few individual polyps. Polyp 1-2cm with long body column, broad oral disk. On coral rubble. Commonly seen on many of our Southern shores. Colony10-15cm, encrusting small stones and rubble sometimes forming dense carpet. Polyps about 1cm with long body column, oral disk appears to be split into two. Commonly seen on many of our shores.    

how to tell apart animals with a ring of smooth tentacles
